heroOverview: Polytrichia (ICD-10: L68.3) is a localised form of hypertrichosis in which one or more patches of skin produce an abnormally dense number of hair follicles or hair shafts, often appearing as a circumscribed tuft of coarse or fine hair over an otherwise normal-looking skin area. Unlike generalised hypertrichosis — which affects broad body regions — polytrichia is restricted to defined patches and may be congenital or acquired. The condition is rare; precise population prevalence is unknown, but it is seen in both sexes and all age groups, with congenital cases often discovered in infancy or early childhood. Spinal or lumbosacral polytrichia can be an external marker of an underlying neural tube defect or spinal dysraphism, making accurate evaluation essential. Most isolated cases carry no systemic implications and are managed cosmetically, but thorough assessment is warranted when the hair tuft sits over the spine or scalp.

keyStats.prevalence: Exact prevalence unknown; polytrichia is classified as a rare hair disorder under ICD-10 L68.3. Lumbosacral hairy patches are reported in approximately 3-8% of cases referred for spinal dysraphism evaluation.
keyStats.demographics: Affects males and females equally in congenital forms; acquired cases may show female predominance when associated with hormonal or drug causes
keyStats.avgAge: Congenital cases identified at birth or early infancy; acquired cases can arise at any age
keyStats.globalCases: No reliable global incidence data; recognised as uncommon in dermatology practice worldwide

definition: Polytrichia (ICD-10: L68.3) refers to localised excess hair follicle density or hair shaft number within a circumscribed skin patch, classified under the group of hypertrichoses. The term derives from the Greek poly (many) and thrix (hair). Mechanistically, polytrichia results from one of three processes: an abnormally high density of hair follicles programmed during embryonic development (congenital follicular excess), a shift of vellus follicles into prolonged or exaggerated anagen (growth phase) producing more visible terminal hair, or local signalling abnormalities — including androgen receptor upregulation, prostaglandin pathway dysregulation, or trauma-induced follicular stimulation — that recruit additional follicles into active cycling. Unlike hirsutism (ICD-10: L68.0), which is androgen-mediated excess hair in women in a male pattern distribution, polytrichia is site-specific and not necessarily androgen driven. Unlike hypertrichosis lanuginosa, it does not involve lanugo-type hair across the body. The condition falls under dermatology; cases over the spine are co-managed with neurosurgery or paediatric surgery. Histologically, an increased follicle count per unit area of dermis is the defining finding.

typesClassification.description: Present from birth, often as an isolated finding or as a surface marker of spinal cord tethering, occult spinal dysraphism, or a dermal sinus tract. The hair tuft commonly sits in the lumbosacral midline.
Develops after birth in response to local stimuli such as chronic trauma, friction, repeated shaving, skin irritation from topical agents, or prolonged contact with prosthetics or casts.
Focal or more widespread follicular activation driven by topical or systemic agents — most notably minoxidil, cyclosporine, phenytoin, or corticosteroid-containing creams applied long-term.
A hamartomatous patch of increased follicle density occurring as a circumscribed, stable, non-progressive nevus, histologically confirmed by excess follicular units.

primarySymptoms: A well-defined patch of noticeably denser, longer, or darker hair than the surrounding skin, most often found on the scalp, lumbosacral midline, trunk, or limbs.
Hair within the affected area that may be coarser, thicker in calibre, or more deeply pigmented than the patient's normal body hair, contrasting visually with adjacent skin.
A circumscribed skin zone that otherwise appears normal — no erythema, scaling, or follicular inflammation in uncomplicated polytrichia.
In spinal polytrichia, a midline lumbosacral tuft that may be associated with a subtle skin dimple, pit, or sinus tract immediately beneath or adjacent to the hair.
Occasional mild local pruritus where hair shafts curl back and cause mechanical irritation of the follicular orifice.
In acquired cases, a history of antecedent local trauma, friction, or topical medication application at the site of increased hair growth.
In drug-induced cases, increased hair density that first appears at application sites and gradually intensifies over weeks to months of continued drug exposure.
Psychosocial distress — visible hair tufts on cosmetically prominent areas such as the face, scalp, or hands cause significant anxiety, especially in adolescents and young adults.
In nevoid polytrichia, a stable patch present since childhood that has not changed size or hair character over many years.
earlyWarningSigns: A new or enlarging patch of dense hair in a previously normal skin area, particularly if appearing after starting a new topical or systemic medication.
A midline lumbosacral hair tuft identified in a neonate or infant, which should prompt imaging to exclude spinal cord tethering.
A hair-bearing skin dimple or pit along the spine that leaks fluid or becomes intermittently inflamed, suggesting a dermal sinus.
Associated numbness, leg weakness, or bladder or bowel dysfunction when the hair tuft is lumbosacral — these neurological signs indicate possible spinal involvement.
emergencySigns: Neurological symptoms — new leg weakness, loss of bladder or bowel control, or saddle anaesthesia — in a patient with lumbosacral polytrichia, indicating possible spinal cord tethering requiring urgent imaging.
Signs of meningitis (neck stiffness, photophobia, fever) accompanying a lumbosacral or scalp hair tuft with an underlying sinus tract, suggesting intracranial or intraspinal communication.
Rapid expansion of a previously stable hair patch with associated skin thickening, ulceration, or subcutaneous mass, which must be evaluated to exclude malignancy.

causes.description: During skin morphogenesis, signalling molecules including WNT, BMP, and sonic hedgehog regulate follicle placode density. Localised dysregulation programmes an abnormally high follicle count in a circumscribed dermal zone, producing a stable congenital hair tuft detectable from birth.

causes.cause: Spinal dysraphism and neural tube defects
causes.description: Defective closure of the posterior neural tube creates mesenchymal–ectodermal interactions that stimulate excess follicular density in the overlying skin, explaining the well-recognised association between lumbosacral polytrichia and conditions such as tethered spinal cord, lipomyelomeningocele, and dermal sinus.

Repeated mechanical stimulation — from tight clothing, prosthetic limb contact, or habitual rubbing — upregulates local growth factors including IGF-1 and vascular endothelial growth factor, prolonging anagen phase and increasing the visible hair count in the affected zone over months to years.

Topical or systemic agents including minoxidil (a potassium-channel opener), cyclosporine (a calcineurin inhibitor), phenytoin, and high-potency glucocorticoids applied chronically to skin can shift vellus follicles into terminal anagen by altering prostaglandin balance and androgen receptor sensitivity, producing polytrichia at application sites.

A benign developmental anomaly of the dermis containing an excess of histologically normal hair follicle units, present from birth as a stable, localised, non-malignant tissue excess. It does not reflect systemic endocrine or metabolic disturbance.

Certain inflammatory skin conditions — notably chronic lichen planus, prolonged psoriatic plaques, or scarring folliculitis — can paradoxically stimulate remaining follicles in a healing zone to enter prolonged anagen, producing denser regrowth in the resolving skin area.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of hypertrichosis or hair growth disorders
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives with documented hypertrichosis syndromes raise the likelihood of congenital polytrichia due to shared genetic variants affecting follicular programming during embryogenesis.

riskFactors.factor: Long-term use of minoxidil or cyclosporine
riskFactors.description: Both agents are well-established causes of localised and generalised increased hair density; topical minoxidil applied chronically can produce focal polytrichia at application sites.

Chronic mechanical irritation from ill-fitting clothing, prostheses, or habitual rubbing at a specific skin site predisposes to localised follicular stimulation and acquired polytrichia over months to years.
Infants born with occult spinal dysraphism or tethered cord are at higher risk of displaying lumbosacral polytrichia as a cutaneous marker of the underlying neural defect.

Long-standing plaque psoriasis, lichen planus, or chronic folliculitis at a localised site can create a pro-anagen microenvironment that drives focal hair excess during and after the inflammatory phase.
High-potency topical steroids applied repeatedly to the same skin site alter local hormonal signalling and can, over time, induce focal hypertrichosis including polytrichia at the application area.
Patients with organoid or connective tissue naevi have a higher incidence of hamartomatous follicular excess within the naevus, which may present clinically as localised polytrichia.

affectedDemographics: Neonates and infants, in whom congenital lumbosacral polytrichia may signal spinal cord tethering
Children and adolescents with stable congenital hair tufts who seek cosmetic assessment
Adults using long-term topical minoxidil or cyclosporine at specific application sites
Patients with chronic plaque psoriasis or lichen planus involving localised skin areas
Individuals in occupations or activities associated with repeated skin friction (prosthetic limb users, manual labourers with tool contact)
Males and females equally for congenital forms; slightly more females for drug-induced and inflammatory forms mirroring the populations using relevant medications
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of polytrichia begins with a targeted history — age of onset, location, associated medications, family history, and any neurological symptoms — followed by careful clinical inspection of the affected patch. A dermatologist characterises the hair calibre, density, pigmentation, and follicular architecture and notes whether the surrounding skin appears normal. Dermoscopy increases resolution of the follicular pattern and helps distinguish a high-density terminal hair area from inflammatory folliculitis or scarring. For lumbosacral or midline scalp polytrichia in children, spinal MRI is the gold-standard investigation to exclude tethered cord, lipomyelomeningocele, dermal sinus, or other spinal dysraphism — ultrasound of the spine is used in neonates as a first-line screen before fontanelle closure makes MRI alone practicable. Skin biopsy is not usually needed for clinical diagnosis but confirms elevated follicle density per unit area and identifies any hamartomatous architecture, inflammatory aetiology, or dermal naevus when the clinical picture is ambiguous. Hormonal workup (testosterone, DHEAS, prolactin, thyroid function) is considered when polytrichia is extensive or the clinical context raises concern for systemic endocrine disease, to distinguish it from androgen-mediated hirsutism. Drug history is reviewed systematically in all acquired cases. Photography with standardised lighting documents the extent for monitoring response to treatment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterises hair density, calibre, pigmentation, and follicular pattern within the patch; distinguishes polytrichia from inflammatory alopecia or folliculitis; identifies associated skin dimples or sinuses.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The dermatologist examines the hair tuft under bright light and a handheld dermoscope; no discomfort; takes about 5-10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Spinal MRI (for lumbosacral or midline polytrichia)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard imaging to detect tethered spinal cord, lipomyelomeningocele, dermal sinus tract, or other occult spinal dysraphism underlying a lumbosacral hair tuft.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still inside a large magnet for 30-60 minutes; no radiation; children may need sedation; a contrast agent is occasionally injected.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line screening for spinal dysraphism in neonates before 3 months of age when the posterior elements are still largely cartilaginous and accessible to ultrasound.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A gel is applied to the lower back and a probe moved gently over the skin; completely painless; takes 15-20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms elevated follicle density per unit area, identifies hamartomatous or naevoid architecture, and excludes inflammatory or neoplastic processes when the diagnosis is uncertain.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Local anaesthetic is injected; a small circular or flat sample of skin is taken; results are available in 7-14 days; a small scar is left.

diagnosticTests.test: Hormonal panel (testosterone, DHEAS, prolactin, thyroid function)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes androgen excess, hyperprolactinaemia, and thyroid dysfunction as systemic drivers of localised or widespread hair increase, particularly when hypertrichosis is extensive.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard venous blood draw; results typically available within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents the distribution, size, and hair character of the affected patch over time to assess natural history and treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Photographs taken under standardised lighting and magnification; non-invasive and painless.

treatmentOverview: For the majority of isolated, cosmetically stable polytrichia patches with no underlying systemic or neurological cause, treatment is purely elective and focuses on hair removal or camouflage rather than any pharmacological intervention. In cases associated with spinal dysraphism, the clinical priority is neurosurgical correction of the underlying anomaly — the overlying hair tuft itself often thins or partially regresses after successful spinal cord untethering. When polytrichia is drug-induced, discontinuing or substituting the causative agent is the first and most effective step, frequently reversing the excess hair growth over 3-6 months without additional intervention. For cosmetic management, laser hair reduction (diode, alexandrite, or Nd:YAG devices depending on skin tone) is the preferred approach for patches larger than a few square centimetres, achieving approximately 70-90% long-term hair reduction over 6-10 sessions. Intense pulsed light (IPL) treats broader surface areas per session and is effective for medium-sized patches in lighter skin types. Electrolysis — permanent needle-based individual follicle destruction — is best reserved for small areas or maintenance after bulk laser reduction. Eflornithine 13.9% cream applied twice daily slows regrowth rate by roughly 30-40% and is used as an adjunct between sessions rather than a standalone treatment. Temporary options such as waxing, threading, and depilatory creams offer quick results but do not reduce follicle density and carry a risk of folliculitis with repeated use. Because polytrichia carries no malignant potential in isolation, watchful waiting is entirely appropriate for patients who find the patch acceptable. When the condition causes significant psychological distress — particularly in children and adolescents with cosmetically prominent patches — early dermatological management substantially reduces psychosocial burden. There is no approved systemic medication specifically targeting localised polytrichia; systemic antiandrogens are not indicated unless confirmed androgen excess is demonstrated on hormonal testing.

medicalTreatments.name: Laser hair reduction (diode, Nd:YAG, alexandrite)
medicalTreatments.description: Pulsed laser energy targets melanin in the hair shaft and papilla, thermally damaging follicles to achieve long-term hair reduction within the polytrichia patch. Multiple sessions (typically 6-10) are required for approximately 70-90% reduction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Long-term hair reduction achieved in approximately 70-90% of treated follicles after a full course; FDA-cleared for permanent hair reduction across all relevant laser platforms.

medicalTreatments.name: Intense pulsed light (IPL)
medicalTreatments.description: Broad-spectrum light in the 500-1200 nm range targets follicular melanin similarly to laser but treats a wider skin area per pulse; effective for large polytrichia patches, particularly in patients with lighter skin tones.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces hair density by approximately 60-80% after 4-6 sessions; somewhat less selective than dedicated laser devices.

medicalTreatments.name: Electrolysis (needle epilation)
medicalTreatments.description: A fine probe is inserted into each follicle individually to deliver thermal or galvanic current, permanently destroying the follicle. Best for very small polytrichia patches or finalising treatment after laser has reduced bulk hair.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves permanent destruction of treated follicles (near 100% in properly treated units); very time-consuming for large patches.

medicalTreatments.name: Eflornithine hydrochloride 13.9% cream
medicalTreatments.description: A topical ornithine decarboxylase inhibitor that slows the rate of hair growth by reducing cell division in the hair follicle matrix; applied twice daily to the affected area. It does not remove existing hair and must be used alongside a removal method.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces hair regrowth rate by approximately 30-40% relative to placebo in clinical trials; maintenance of effect requires continued application.

medicalTreatments.name: Neurosurgical untethering (for spinal dysraphism cases)
medicalTreatments.description: Surgical release of a tethered spinal cord or repair of an associated lipomyelomeningocele removes the neural-ectodermal signalling that sustains lumbosacral polytrichia; the hair tuft may thin or regress over months after successful untethering.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Neurological outcome data show stabilisation or improvement in approximately 80% of patients; regression of overlying hair occurs in a variable proportion post-operatively.

surgicalOptions.name: Spinal cord untethering
surgicalOptions.description: Neurosurgical procedure to release an anomalously anchored spinal cord in cases where lumbosacral polytrichia is a cutaneous marker of tethered cord syndrome or occult spinal dysraphism; primary goal is neurological protection, with secondary benefit of possible hair reduction.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Neurological stabilisation or improvement in approximately 80% of patients; considered the standard of care when tethered cord is confirmed on MRI.

surgicalOptions.name: Dermatological excision
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical excision of the hair-bearing skin patch is occasionally performed for small, well-defined polytrichia nevi that have failed laser therapy or are in a surgically accessible location with acceptable cosmetic defect. Reserved for cases in which other modalities are impractical.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Definitive resolution of hair growth in the excised area; scar formation is the main trade-off; success rate for hair clearance approaches 100% but scar aesthetics vary.

alternativeTreatments.name: Depilatory creams (thioglycolate-based)
alternativeTreatments.description: Chemical depilatories break disulfide bonds in the hair shaft, dissolving it at skin level; fast and painless, but regrowth occurs within days to weeks and repeated use can irritate skin.

alternativeTreatments.name: Waxing and threading
alternativeTreatments.description: Mechanical removal of hair from the follicle mouth; longer-lasting than shaving (3-6 weeks) but do not reduce follicle density and carry a small risk of folliculitis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Camouflage cosmetics
alternativeTreatments.description: Tinted concealers and foundations can reduce the visual contrast of a dark hair tuft on light skin in cosmetically sensitive areas, providing a non-invasive option for patients who decline medical or surgical treatment.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist should evaluate any new or enlarging circumscribed hair tuft to establish whether it is congenital, acquired, or drug-induced, and to perform dermoscopy and arrange appropriate imaging. Any child with a midline lumbosacral hair patch should be seen by paediatric neurosurgery to assess for spinal cord tethering before neurological deficits develop — early untethering prevents irreversible bladder, bowel, or motor impairment. When patients seek permanent hair removal, dermatologist-supervised laser or electrolysis achieves significantly better and safer outcomes than over-the-counter devices.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a dermatologist with experience in laser hair reduction and hair follicle disorders, ideally with access to multiple laser platforms (diode, Nd:YAG, alexandrite) to match the device to your skin and hair type. For lumbosacral polytrichia in a child, a paediatric neurosurgeon with spinal dysraphism subspecialty is essential. Ask whether the provider performs dermoscopy routinely and whether they have a protocol for ordering spinal imaging when indicated.
preventionStrategies: Avoid prolonged use of topical minoxidil, cyclosporine, or high-potency corticosteroids on skin areas where focal hair growth is undesirable; discuss alternatives with your prescriber.
Reduce chronic skin friction at high-contact sites (prosthetic limb interfaces, tight waistbands) by ensuring proper fit and using protective padding.
For any infant with a midline lumbosacral hair tuft identified at birth, prompt paediatric assessment and spinal ultrasound reduce the risk of missed spinal dysraphism.
Genetic counselling is appropriate for families with a history of congenital hypertrichosis syndromes before further pregnancies.
lifestyleModifications: Review all medications (topical and systemic) with your dermatologist if a new hair patch appears, as discontinuation of the causative drug is often sufficient.
Use gentle hair removal methods at affected sites to avoid chronic skin trauma that can perpetuate follicular stimulation.
Protect cosmetically treated areas from sun exposure immediately after laser sessions to prevent post-inflammatory pigmentation.
Maintain a record of hair density changes using photographs to guide treatment decisions and identify spontaneous regression.

recommended: A balanced diet adequate in protein and micronutrients (iron, zinc, biotin) to support healthy hair cycling — deficiencies can complicate assessment by affecting hair quality across the body
Anti-inflammatory foods (oily fish, leafy greens, berries) may modestly support skin health, though no specific dietary intervention modifies polytrichia
avoid: No specific dietary restrictions are required for polytrichia; excessively restrictive diets can cause telogen effluvium that complicates the clinical picture

exerciseGuidelines: Regular physical activity is fully compatible with polytrichia and its treatment. Following laser hair reduction sessions, strenuous exercise causing heavy sweating should be avoided for 24-48 hours to reduce the risk of post-treatment folliculitis or skin irritation at the treated site. No other exercise restrictions apply.
dailyManagement: Apply eflornithine cream twice daily if prescribed, to clean dry skin; do not wash the treated area for at least 4 hours after application.
Protect laser-treated areas from sun exposure using SPF 30+ sunscreen for at least four weeks after each session.
Monitor lumbosacral hair tufts for any change in skin texture, new dimple, or associated neurological symptoms, and report these promptly.
Track response to hair removal treatment with periodic photographs under consistent lighting conditions.
Contact your dermatologist if any hair patch grows rapidly or changes character over a short period, as this warrants re-evaluation.
prognosis: Isolated polytrichia without an underlying systemic cause carries an excellent long-term prognosis. The condition is benign, non-malignant, and non-progressive in most cases. Cosmetic laser hair reduction achieves approximately 70-90% permanent hair density reduction over a full course of sessions, and many patients are satisfied with the cosmetic outcome. Drug-induced polytrichia reverses fully in the majority of patients after the causative agent is discontinued, typically over 3-6 months. For lumbosacral polytrichia linked to spinal dysraphism, prognosis for the hair patch is secondary to the neurological outlook — early surgical untethering before the development of deficits yields stabilisation or improvement in approximately 80% of patients. No cases of malignant transformation of polytrichia have been reported in the dermatology literature.
recoveryTimeline: After laser hair reduction sessions spaced 4-8 weeks apart, treated hairs shed within 1-3 weeks and visible density reduction accumulates over 6-10 sessions (roughly 6-12 months of treatment). Drug-induced polytrichia typically reverses within 3-6 months of stopping the offending agent. Recovery from neurosurgical spinal untethering requires 4-6 weeks for wound healing, with neurological assessment at 3-6 months post-operatively.
complications: Missed spinal dysraphism — overlooking lumbosacral polytrichia as a marker of tethered cord can allow progressive neurological deficits (bladder dysfunction, leg weakness) to develop that might have been prevented by early surgery.

Post-laser dyspigmentation — laser hair reduction on darker skin types (Fitzpatrick IV-VI) carries a risk of temporary or permanent hypopigmentation or hyperpigmentation in the treated zone if incorrect wavelength, fluence, or pulse duration is used.

Folliculitis and bacterial superinfection — repeated waxing, threading, or depilatory use can disrupt the follicular barrier, allowing Staphylococcus aureus colonisation and folliculitis within the polytrichia patch.
Psychosocial impact — visible hair tufts on the face, neck, or hands cause significant distress; body dysmorphic tendencies can develop in adolescents if the condition is not addressed.
Scar formation — surgical excision of the hair-bearing patch leaves a permanent scar; the cosmetic trade-off should be carefully weighed against the severity of the polytrichia.

sources.title: Wendelin DS, Pope DN, Mallory SB. Hypertrichosis. Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology, 2003
Trüeb RM. Causes and management of hypertrichosis. American Journal of Clinical Dermatology, 2002

confusedWithConditions.name: Hirsutism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hirsutism is androgen-mediated excess hair in women in a male-pattern distribution (face, chest, abdomen) and is associated with polycystic ovary syndrome or adrenal disease, whereas polytrichia is a localised patch not driven by androgens.

confusedWithConditions.name: Alopecia universalis (context: hair excess vs. loss)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Alopecia universalis presents with total hair loss, not excess; it can be confused with polytrichia only during regrowth phases when isolated tufts appear before generalised regrowth is complete.

confusedWithConditions.name: Syphilitic alopecia (moth-eaten pattern)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Secondary syphilis causes patchy hair loss with a moth-eaten appearance; a residual tuft of intact hair within a hypopigmented patch can superficially resemble polytrichia until serological testing clarifies the diagnosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Other congenital hair malformations
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Congenital hair malformations such as pili torti or monilethrix affect hair shaft structure across the body rather than producing a localised dense patch, and are distinguished by trichoscopy of individual shaft morphology.

relatedConditions.name: Hirsutism
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are classified under ICD-10 L68 as hypertrichoses and share diagnostic overlap; distinguishing polytrichia from hirsutism guides appropriate investigation and management.

relatedConditions.name: Androgenic alopecia
relatedConditions.relevance: Androgen-mediated hair cycling underlies androgenic alopecia; understanding the opposite end of this spectrum aids in characterising drug-induced or hormonal polytrichia.

relatedConditions.name: Other congenital hair malformations
relatedConditions.relevance: Congenital polytrichia is classified within the broader group of embryological hair formation anomalies, sharing pathways with other congenital follicular dysplasias.

relatedConditions.name: Rosacea
relatedConditions.relevance: Facial rosacea affecting hair-bearing areas of the face requires differential evaluation when increased facial hair density accompanies erythema, since anti-rosacea treatments (topical azelaic acid, metronidazole) differ from hair-reduction regimens.

faqs.question: What is polytrichia?
faqs.answer: Polytrichia is a rare localised skin condition where one patch of skin grows an abnormally high density of hair follicles or hair shafts. Unlike generalised excess body hair, it is confined to a well-defined area.

faqs.question: Is polytrichia the same as hypertrichosis?
faqs.answer: Polytrichia is a specific subtype of hypertrichosis. Hypertrichosis is the broader term for excess hair anywhere on the body; polytrichia specifically refers to localised follicular excess in a circumscribed patch rather than a diffuse increase.

faqs.question: What causes a localised tuft of dense hair on the lower back?
faqs.answer: A midline lumbosacral hair tuft is often a marker of occult spinal dysraphism — an incomplete closure of the spinal column. It can also result from a congenital follicular naevus. Any such tuft in a child should be evaluated with spinal imaging.

faqs.question: Is polytrichia dangerous?
faqs.answer: Isolated polytrichia is benign and non-malignant. However, a lumbosacral hair tuft can signal a tethered spinal cord, which does require assessment and sometimes surgery to prevent neurological problems. Cosmetic patches without spinal involvement carry no health risk.

faqs.question: How is polytrichia treated?
faqs.answer: Laser hair reduction, electrolysis, intense pulsed light, and eflornithine cream are the main treatment options. Drug-induced cases often resolve when the causative medication is stopped. Lumbosacral cases may need neurosurgical correction of an underlying spinal abnormality.

faqs.question: Can minoxidil cause polytrichia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Topical minoxidil applied chronically to skin can produce focal polytrichia at the application site by prolonging the hair growth phase in vellus follicles. Reducing or stopping minoxidil use usually reverses the excess hair growth within 3 to 6 months.

faqs.question: Does laser hair removal work for polytrichia?
faqs.answer: Laser hair reduction achieves approximately 70 to 90 percent long-term reduction in hair density after a full course of 6 to 10 sessions. Multiple sessions spaced 4 to 8 weeks apart are required. The laser type is selected based on skin tone to minimise the risk of pigment changes.

faqs.question: Is polytrichia hereditary?
faqs.answer: Congenital forms can run in families with known hypertrichosis syndromes, suggesting a genetic component in follicular programming. However, most isolated cases appear sporadically. A family history of hypertrichosis increases the likelihood but is not required for diagnosis.

faqs.question: At what age does polytrichia appear?
faqs.answer: Congenital polytrichia is present from birth and typically noticed in infancy. Acquired forms can appear at any age following chronic skin trauma, drug exposure, or inflammatory skin disease. Drug-induced cases usually develop after weeks to months of continued medication use.

faqs.question: What is the difference between polytrichia and hirsutism?
faqs.answer: Hirsutism is androgen-driven excess hair in women in male-pattern locations (chin, upper lip, chest, lower abdomen) and is associated with conditions like polycystic ovary syndrome. Polytrichia is a localised patch of dense hair not caused by androgen excess and not restricted to any sex or body region.

faqs.question: Should I get an MRI for a hair tuft on my back?
faqs.answer: A midline lumbosacral or sacral hair tuft in a child or infant should be evaluated with spinal ultrasound (under 3 months) or MRI (older children and adults) to exclude tethered spinal cord or spinal dysraphism, particularly if there is also a skin dimple, pit, or any neurological symptom.

faqs.question: Can polytrichia go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Drug-induced polytrichia often resolves spontaneously over 3 to 6 months once the causative agent is stopped. Congenital polytrichia does not regress without treatment. Post-inflammatory polytrichia may slowly fade as the skin heals, but usually requires active management for cosmetically significant cases.

faqs.question: Is eflornithine cream effective for polytrichia?
faqs.answer: Eflornithine 13.9% cream slows hair regrowth rate by about 30 to 40 percent in treated areas by inhibiting an enzyme in the hair follicle matrix. It does not remove existing hair and works best when combined with laser or physical hair removal methods.

faqs.question: Does shaving make polytrichia worse?
faqs.answer: Shaving does not increase follicle density or make polytrichia worse in the long term; hair appears stubbly after shaving because it is cut bluntly rather than tapered. However, repeated shaving can cause mechanical skin irritation that may temporarily worsen follicular inflammation in a susceptible patch.

faqs.question: Which specialist treats polytrichia?
faqs.answer: A dermatologist manages most cases of polytrichia, performing dermoscopy and overseeing laser or electrolysis treatment. When lumbosacral polytrichia is associated with spinal dysraphism, a paediatric neurosurgeon or spinal surgeon is essential. Endocrinology is involved if hormonal excess is suspected.

faqs.question: How many laser sessions are needed for polytrichia?
faqs.answer: Most dermatologists plan 6 to 10 sessions spaced 4 to 8 weeks apart to achieve around 70 to 90 percent long-term hair reduction. Maintenance sessions once or twice yearly may be needed thereafter, as a small proportion of follicles can re-enter anagen over time.

faqs.question: Can polytrichia affect any part of the body?
faqs.answer: Yes. Polytrichia can occur anywhere on the body with hair-bearing skin. The most clinically significant sites are the lumbosacral midline (due to spinal association), scalp, and cosmetically prominent areas such as the face, neck, and hands.

faqs.question: What does dermoscopy show in polytrichia?
faqs.answer: Dermoscopy of a polytrichia patch typically shows an increased number of follicular openings per unit area, with terminal or coarse hair shafts exiting from normal-looking follicular infundibula. There is no perifollicular fibrosis, as seen in scarring alopecias, and no inflammatory signs unless secondary folliculitis is present.

faqs.question: Is polytrichia associated with cancer?
faqs.answer: No malignant transformation of polytrichia has been reported. However, a rapidly growing, changing, or tender hair-bearing patch with skin thickening or an underlying mass warrants biopsy to exclude a primary skin neoplasm or metastatic deposit that may secondarily stimulate hair follicles.

faqs.question: Can polytrichia affect children's quality of life?
faqs.answer: Visible hair tufts — especially on the face, scalp, or hands — can cause significant self-consciousness and social anxiety in school-age children and adolescents. Early dermatological treatment is generally recommended when the condition affects cosmetically prominent sites, as it reduces psychosocial burden before social patterns are established.

metaDescription: Polytrichia is localised excess hair follicle density in a defined skin patch. Learn causes, spinal links, laser treatment and when to seek specialist care.
